jsonLdWeb.name: Hay Bale Calculator
jsonLdWeb.description: Calculate hay bale requirements for cattle, horses, goats, and sheep based on herd size and feeding period.

mainEntity.name: How much hay does a cow eat per day?

acceptedAnswer.text: An average 1,200-lb cow eats about 25-30 lbs of hay per day, or roughly 2.5% of body weight. Lactating cows may eat up to 35 lbs. Over a 180-day winter feeding season, one cow consumes about 4,500-5,400 lbs of hay.

mainEntity.name: How many bales of hay per cow per winter?

acceptedAnswer.text: For a 180-day feeding period: about 108 small square bales (50 lbs each), 5-6 large square bales (1,000 lbs), or 4-5 round bales (1,200 lbs). Add 10-15% for waste depending on feeder type.

mainEntity.name: How much hay does a horse eat per day?

acceptedAnswer.text: An average 1,000-lb horse eats 15-20 lbs of hay per day, about 1.5-2% of body weight. Horses in heavy work may need more. Quality matters — horses need cleaner, dust-free hay than cattle.

mainEntity.name: How much does a round bale of hay weigh?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 5×5 round bale weighs 1,000-1,200 lbs for grass hay. A 4×4 round bale weighs 500-700 lbs. Alfalfa round bales can weigh 15-20% more than grass hay bales of the same size.

mainEntity.name: How much storage space do hay bales need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Small square bales need about 6 sq ft each stacked 5 high. Large square bales need about 24 sq ft each. Round bales need about 36 sq ft each and should not be stacked more than 2 high. Allow airflow between rows.

mainEntity.name: How do I reduce hay waste?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use hay rings or feeders to reduce waste from 30-40% (ground feeding) to 5-15%. Store hay under cover on pallets. Feed smaller amounts more frequently. Round bale feeders with cone-style covers waste only 3-5%.

howItWorks:
How the Hay Bale Calculator Works

This calculator estimates total hay needs based on animal type, herd size, and feeding period, then converts to bale counts.

Total Hay = Animals × Lbs/Day × Days × (1 + Waste%)
  • Cattle: ~30 lbs/day (2.5% of body weight)
  • Horses: ~20 lbs/day (2% of body weight)
  • Goats/Sheep: ~5 lbs/day (3% of body weight)
  • Bale sizes: Small square 50 lbs, large square 1,000 lbs, round 1,200 lbs
  • Waste factor: 5-15% with feeders, 25-40% ground feeding
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How much hay does a cow eat per day?

An average 1,200-lb cow eats 25-30 lbs of hay per day, roughly 2.5% of body weight. Lactating cows or cattle in cold weather may eat up to 35 lbs. Over a 180-day winter, one cow needs about 5,400 lbs of hay.

How many bales per cow per winter?

For a 180-day feeding season, plan on about 108 small square bales (50 lbs each), 5-6 large square bales (1,000 lbs), or 4-5 round bales (1,200 lbs) per cow. Add your waste factor on top.

How much hay does a horse eat per day?

An average 1,000-lb horse eats 15-20 lbs of hay per day, about 1.5-2% of body weight. Use clean, dust-free hay for horses, as they are more sensitive to mold and dust than cattle.

How much does a round bale weigh?

A standard 5x5 round bale weighs 1,000-1,200 lbs for grass hay and up to 1,400 lbs for dense alfalfa. A smaller 4x4 round bale weighs 500-700 lbs. Weight varies with moisture and density.

How much storage space do hay bales need?

Round bales need about 36 sq ft each on the ground. Small squares need 6 sq ft stacked 5 high. Store under cover on pallets with airflow between rows to prevent mold and spoilage.

How do I reduce hay waste?

Use hay rings or feeders to cut waste from 30-40% (ground feeding) to 5-15%. Cone-style round bale feeders limit waste to 3-5%. Feed smaller portions more frequently and keep hay dry and covered.
